text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST AND MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
57-year-old female with known Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, primary site left posterior shoulder. History of prior treatment with dabrafenib/trametinib (progressed at approximately 8 months), followed by ipilimumab/nivolumab combination induction (4 doses, course complicated by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring ipilimumab discontinuation and high-dose corticosteroids), followed by rechallenge and ongoing nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance monotherapy. Most recent restaging CT approximately 6 months prior demonstrated deepening partial response with bilateral axillary lymph nodes approaching normal limits, near-resolution of hepatic metastases, and disappearance of previously identified pulmonary nodules. Patient now presents for scheduled 12-month maintenance restaging. LDH reported at 162 U/L (normal range per institutional lab). Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 6 months prior; MRI brain with and without contrast performed approximately 6 months prior.

---

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Helical acquisition performed from the lung apices through the symphysis pubis following administration of 100 mL of iohexol 350 mg I/mL intravenous contrast, injected via right antecubital vein at 3 mL/sec. Scans obtained in the portal venous phase for abdomen/pelvis and in the delayed phase for chest. 3 mm axial reconstructions with coronal and sagittal reformats. No oral contrast administered. Radiation dose within institutional diagnostic reference levels.

MRI brain: Multiplanar, multi-sequence brain MRI performed on a 3-Tesla system. Sequences obtained: sagittal T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial DWI/ADC, axial T2* gradient echo, and axial T1-weighted pre- and post-gadolinium contrast (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg IV). High-resolution post-contrast 3D T1-weighted volumetric acquisition (MPRAGE) obtained for lesion characterization. No significant susceptibility artifact. No prior MRI brain for comparison at this institution beyond the study obtained 6 months ago.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The central airways are patent. The trachea is midline and unobstructed. The mainstem bronchi are widely patent bilaterally. The lungs are clear. There are no parenchymal nodules, masses, or consolidations identified. On the prior examination approximately 6 months ago, two small nodules were present in the right lower lobe measuring up to 3 mm; these are no longer visualized on the current study, falling below CT resolution. No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No ground-glass opacities, tree-in-bud pattern, or interstitial changes are appreciated. No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size with no pericardial effusion. The aorta and great vessels are normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilatation or filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thymus is not enlarged. The esophagus is unremarkable in appearance.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues:
No chest wall masses, abnormal soft tissue nodules, or subcutaneous lesions. The previously identified multiple hypermetabolic subcutaneous nodules in the bilateral axillary regions and right flank that were present at initial staging are no longer seen. No cutaneous thickening or ulceration.

Axillae (Bilateral):
Right axilla: A small residual lymph node is present measuring approximately 0.4 cm in short axis. This has maintained stability compared to the prior examination (previously measured 0.5 cm), and is within normal size limits. It retains normal hilar fatty architecture. No morphologic features concerning for malignant involvement.
Left axilla: A small lymph node measures approximately 0.3 cm in short axis, unchanged from the prior study (previously 0.3 cm), and is not enlarged. Normal fatty hilum preserved. No additional pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in either axilla.

Bones (Chest):
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified in the visualized thoracic spine, ribs, clavicles, or shoulder girdles. Degenerative changes are noted at several thoracic vertebral levels, most prominently at T7-T8, without associated soft tissue component or epidural involvement.

CT ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and morphology. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous on pre- and post-contrast sequences without evidence of focal mass, nodule, or infiltrative process. On the prior examination, two residual hypodense lesions were present in the right hepatic lobe, the larger measuring 0.6 cm and a smaller 0.3 cm lesion. On the current study, the previously identified 0.6 cm lesion in the right lobe now appears as a tiny focus of hypoattenuation measuring approximately 0.4 cm, representing continued interval decrease in size. This lesion shows no appreciable enhancement on portal venous phase images and no arterial phase hyperenhancement. The previously noted second right lobe lesion measuring 0.3 cm is no longer visualized. No new hepatic lesions are identified. The hepatic vasculature including portal vein and hepatic veins are patent and of normal caliber. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present, with no wall thickening, stones, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct measures 4 mm in caliber, within normal limits.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size (craniocaudal span approximately 11.2 cm) and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size, contour, and attenuation throughout head, neck, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid collection.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodules, masses, or enlargement. No adrenal metastases.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size (right kidney 11.1 cm, left kidney 11.3 cm craniocaudal) with preserved corticomedullary differentiation and normal enhancement. No hydronephrosis, hydroureter, or perirenal stranding. A 4 mm simple renal cyst is noted in the right lower pole, unchanged from prior examination. No solid renal masses.

Stomach and Small Bowel:
The stomach is decompressed without wall thickening or mucosal abnormality. Loops of small bowel demonstrate normal caliber and wall thickness throughout. No obstruction, ileus, or free fluid to suggest perforation or peritonitis. No mesenteric edema. Given the patient's prior history of grade 3 immune-mediated colitis treated with high-dose corticosteroids, the small bowel mucosa is scrutinized carefully and is unremarkable.

Colon:
The colon is not opacified with oral contrast; however, no wall thickening, pericolonic fat stranding, or pericolic fluid is identified. The descending colon, site of prior immune-related colitis (as documented on prior flexible sigmoidoscopy), demonstrates no mural thickening, no haustral irregularity, and no abnormal surrounding fat stranding. No pneumoperitoneum.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
No peritoneal implants, omental thickening, or ascites. No mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Mesenteric fat is normal in attenuation.

Retroperitoneum:
No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The aorta is normal in caliber. No retroperitoneal mass.

CT PELVIS:

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No inguinal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. Iliac chain lymph nodes are within normal size limits bilaterally.

Pelvic Organs:
The uterus and adnexa appear unremarkable for patient age with no adnexal masses, free fluid, or pelvic soft tissue mass. The urinary bladder is partially distended with no wall thickening or intraluminal lesion. Rectum is normal in caliber.

Bones (Abdomen/Pelvis):
No suspicious osseous lesions in the visualized lumbar spine, sacrum, iliac wings, or proximal femora. Mild degenerative facet disease in the lumbar spine. No pathologic fracture.

Skin and Subcutaneous Tissues:
There are no subcutaneous nodules, skin thickening, or soft tissue masses identified in the abdomen, flanks, or pelvis. The right flank soft tissues, site of prior subcutaneous nodule involvement, are entirely normal.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM:

Brain Parenchyma:
The brain parenchyma is normal in signal intensity on all sequences. No areas of abnormal T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensity to suggest edema, demyelination, or infiltrative process. No diffusion restriction on DWI sequences to suggest acute or subacute ischemia. No areas of abnormal signal on gradient echo/T2* sequences to suggest hemorrhagic metastases or microhemorrhage. Gray-white matter differentiation is well preserved. The cortical gyral pattern is normal for patient age.

Post-Contrast Enhancement:
Following intravenous gadolinium administration, there are no foci of parenchymal enhancement, leptomeningeal enhancement, or pachymeningeal enhancement identified. On high-resolution 3D MPRAGE sequences, no enhancing lesions are identified at any size threshold, including no punctate enhancing foci. The blood-brain barrier appears intact without breakdown to suggest metastatic seeding. No leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

Ventricles and CSF Spaces:
The ventricular system is normal in size and configuration. No hydrocephalus. The sulci and cisterns are preserved and symmetrical, appropriate for patient age without evidence of mass effect or herniation. The basal cisterns are patent.

Extra-Axial Spaces:
No subdural or epidural collections. No extra-axial masses.

Pituitary and Sella:
The pituitary gland is normal in size without hemorrhage, enlargement, or mass lesion. The infundibulum is midline. Given the patient's history of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, the pituitary is carefully evaluated; no hypophysitis or enlargement is identified. This is reassuring in the context of the patient's prior nivolumab therapy.

Cerebral Vasculature:
Flow voids are normal within the major intracranial arteries and dural venous sinuses. No dominant vascular lesion.

Posterior Fossa:
The cerebellum and brainstem are normal in signal, size, and morphology. No posterior fossa lesions. Fourth ventricle is normal.

Orbits and Calvarium:
The visualized orbits are unremarkable. The calvarium demonstrates normal diploic marrow signal without focal signal abnormality to suggest skull metastasis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Solitary residual right hepatic lobe lesion now measuring approximately 0.4 cm, down from 0.6 cm on the prior examination six months ago and significantly reduced compared to the lesion measuring 3.1 cm at initial presentation. The lesion demonstrates no appreciable enhancement and is progressively diminishing in size, representing continued deepening of the treatment response at this site. The second previously identified small right hepatic lesion is no longer seen.

2. Bilateral axillary lymph nodes remain stable within normal size limits (right 0.4 cm, left 0.3 cm), with no morphologic features suggestive of active nodal disease. No new axillary or other regional lymphadenopathy.

3. Previously identified right lower lobe pulmonary nodules are no longer visualized, consistent with ongoing resolution and response.

4. No subcutaneous nodules, peritoneal disease, adrenal metastases, or other new foci of disease identified.

5. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease on dedicated MRI brain with high-resolution post-gadolinium sequences. No leptomeningeal disease.

6. Overall, the imaging picture is one of a sustained and continuing treatment response across all previously involved disease sites, with reduction to a single tiny sub-centimeter hepatic focus as the only remaining radiographic abnormality that could represent residual disease. This constitutes a near-complete imaging response relative to the burden of disease documented at initial staging.

Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist Signature]
Reviewed and Approved
Body Imaging and Oncologic Imaging Section
